summary.overall: The chapter 6 pass 1 staging batch is strong on source fidelity and operational relevance, with no paragraph-location scaffolding, generally well-constructed distractors, and solid cross-format coverage across questions, activities, and flashcards. One critical answer-key contradiction exists in a 6-6-3 activity where the marked-correct choice says 'No' but its own explanation and the source rule require 'Yes.' Several minor wording, duplication, and answer-length-cue issues are present but do not introduce factual errors.
strengths: No paragraph-location scaffolding in question stems — all items use operational context rather than document navigation references.
Correct answers are distributed across choice positions; no systematic first-position bias.
Distractors are generally plausible, parallel, and reflect genuine operational misconceptions rather than nonsense options.
Phraseology items faithfully reproduce the conditional tiers from the source (SAY ALTITUDE / SAY FLIGHT LEVEL / SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL; REPORT LEAVING / REPORT LEAVING ODD/EVEN).
6-5-4 route protection items cover subparagraphs (degree-distance fixes, GNSS random RNAV, impromptu transitions) that have little or no existing content coverage.
Flashcards each target a single coherent retrieval target; list cards are reserved for required memorized sequences or procedures.
Explanations consistently teach the controlling principle rather than restating which paragraph number contains the answer.
6-7-1 activity correctly applies the integrated ceiling-plus-elevation-vs-MDA comparison from the source note.
patterns: Answer-key contradiction in activities.6-6-3.items[0]: the marked-correct choice text says 'No' but internally confirms the authorization is valid. The question asks 'May the controller authorize?' and the rule answer is 'Yes.'

Minor close paraphrasing between questions.6-6-3.items[0] and questions.6-6-3.items[1]: both test the lower-if-climbing / upper-if-descending assignment from different angles with substantial conceptual overlap.
Two multiple-choice questions (6-7-1.items[0] and 6-7-4.items[1]) have correct answers noticeably longer than their distractors, creating mild answer-length cues.
Flashcards.6-5-1.items[0] omits 'or hold at' from the back-side summary of lateral separation method (b), slightly narrowing the method's scope relative to the source.
Fill-in-the-blank format with a single answer choice (questions.6-6-1.items[1]) provides minimal distractor challenge, though it matches the existing content format conventions.
Cross-format reinforcement between questions and flashcards for 6-7-6 (time check rule/exception) and 6-7-4 (level-flight trigger conditions) is appropriate and adds learning value.
No 'paragraph-title trivia' or 'underspecified example checks' found — all items test operational knowledge or justified exact recall.
Three paragraphs in the staging scope (6-5-1, 6-5-4, 6-7-1) add coverage for sub-paragraphs not addressed in existing content, filling genuine gaps.

findings.problem: The marked-correct answer choice says 'No; Aircraft A is the upper aircraft descending toward Aircraft B — this matches the rule's assignment and the authorization is valid.' The leading 'No' contradicts the rest of the sentence and the explanation, which confirm the authorization IS valid. The question asks 'May the controller authorize Aircraft A to maintain vertical separation?' The correct answer is Yes. A student reading this answer would be confused by the internal contradiction.
findings.source_basis: 6-6-3 body: 'When pilots of aircraft in direct radio communication with each other during climb and descent concur, you may authorize the lower aircraft, if climbing, or the upper aircraft, if descending, to maintain vertical separation.' Aircraft A at FL200 is the upper aircraft and is descending toward Aircraft B at FL180. This matches the rule exactly.
findings.recommended_action: Change the leading 'No;' to 'Yes;' in the correct answer choice text so that the choice reads 'Yes; Aircraft A is the upper aircraft descending toward Aircraft B — this matches the rule's assignment and the authorization is valid.'

findings.problem: This question ('Which aircraft is moving toward the other under the rule's assignment?') tests the same operational fact as questions.6-6-3.items[0] ('Which aircraft may the controller authorize to maintain that separation?'). Both answer correctly with lower-if-climbing / upper-if-descending. The conceptual overlap is substantial; items[0] already establishes the rule assignment, and items[1] re-asks it from a movement-direction angle without adding new operational depth.
findings.recommended_action: Consider merging items[0] and items[1] or replacing items[1] with a question that tests a different aspect of 6-6-3, such as the radio-communication requirement or a scenario where the conditions are not met.

findings.problem: The flashcard back summarizes lateral separation method (b) as '(Below 18,000) Proceed to and report over different geographical locations.' The source text reads 'proceed to and report over or hold at different geographical locations.' Omitting 'or hold at' narrows the method's scope by removing an entire permissible alternative (holding at geographical locations, distinct from method (c) which covers holding over different fixes).
findings.source_basis: 6-5-1 body: 'b. Clear aircraft below 18,000 to proceed to and report over or hold at different geographical locations determined visually or by reference to NAVAIDs.'
findings.recommended_action: Add 'or hold at' to the back text so method (b) reads '(Below 18,000) Proceed to and report over or hold at different geographical locations.'

findings.problem: The question stem says 'A controller needs to know when an aircraft departs an assigned altitude but is unsure whether the aircraft is above or below the lowest usable flight level.' The phrase 'needs to know when an aircraft departs' can be misread as asking for a REPORT LEAVING phraseology. The intended meaning is that the controller needs to request the aircraft's current altitude in a situation where the aircraft has departed its assigned altitude and the controller is unsure of the FL status. The ambiguity could lead a knowledgeable student to select REPORT LEAVING rather than SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL.
findings.source_basis: 6-6-1 phraseology: 'If aircraft's position relative to the lowest useable flight level is unknown, SAY ALTITUDE OR FLIGHT LEVEL.'
findings.recommended_action: Reword the stem to remove the ambiguity, e.g.: 'A controller needs to request an aircraft's current altitude but is unsure whether the aircraft is above or below the lowest usable flight level. What phraseology should be used?' This makes clear the controller is requesting altitude information, not a report of altitude change.

findings.problem: The correct answer choice (155 characters) is substantially longer than all three distractors (51, 68, and 63 characters respectively). The correct answer restates the full source-note capability language while distractors are concise statements. This length asymmetry creates a mild answer-length cue.
findings.recommended_action: Trim the correct answer to be comparable in length to distractors, e.g.: 'Hold at a fix on the approach course or be radar vectored to the final approach course per interval minima.'

findings.problem: The correct answer (132 characters) is roughly twice the length of the three distractors (65, 51, and 72 characters). This creates a mild answer-length cue.
findings.recommended_action: Shorten the correct answer while preserving accuracy, e.g.: 'When 6-7-2b is applied AND IFR conditions are reported over the final approach fix or outer marker.'

findings.problem: This is a fill-in-the-blank item with a single answer choice, which provides no distractor challenge. While the existing content corpus also uses single-answer fill_blank items, a format that requires the student to produce or select from competing alternatives would better assess recall.
findings.recommended_action: Consider converting to multiple_choice with plausible distractors (e.g., SAY ALTITUDE, SAY FLIGHT LEVEL, REPORT LEAVING) to test discrimination between the three conditional phraseologies rather than isolated recall.

findings.problem: Flashcard items[0] ('When must a time check be issued...') and items[1] ('What is the exception...') together cover the identical rule/exception pair tested by questions.6-7-6.items[0] and questions.6-7-6.items[1]. While cross-format reinforcement is generally desirable, four items testing the same two-fact pair from one brief source sentence approaches over-coverage for a single-sentence rule.
findings.recommended_action: This is a marginal call — the time-check rule is operationally important. If the batch were to be trimmed, consider keeping both questions and one flashcard that captures the full rule-with-exception.
